one_line_takeaway: AURA defends proprietary knowledge graphs against private-use IP theft by injecting a minimal, MVC-selected, hybrid LLM+link-prediction set of plausible-but-false adulterants that drive unauthorized GraphRAG accuracy down to single digits while a secret-key-gated AES metadata filter preserves 100% fidelity and near-zero latency overhead for authorized users.
key_finding: AURA drives Harmfulness Score consistently above 94% across all four datasets and four LLMs (e.g., 95.6% HS on HotpotQA with GPT-4o), with a perfect 100% Adulterant Retrieval Rate in every configuration (Table I), and effectiveness increases with reasoning-chain length (HS rises from 94.7% at 1-hop to 95.8% at 3-hop on MetaQA, Table II). For authorized users, CDPA and CIRA are both a perfect 100% on all four datasets and across 1/2/3-hop questions (Tables III-IV), with total query-time increase of only 1.2%-14.05% (worst case on WebQSP; retrieve-time-only increase up to 45.9% on WebQSP but this is a small absolute latency addition per Table V), and per-flag AES decryption costing only 0.0095 ms. Stealthiness on MetaQA (252,145 injected triples): only 4.1% detected by structural anomaly detection (ODDBALL) and 3.3% by semantic-consistency detection (Node2Vec-based), 5.5% unique detection across both (Table VI). Robustness on MetaQA: SEKA (hybrid structural+semantic attack) removes only 5.5% of adulterants (RR ~94.5% retained per the ablation figure); the TransE-based KGE sanitization attack -- trained on the clean original KG as a deliberately favorable-to-the-attacker setting -- removes 19.8% of adulterants (RR = 80.2% retained), after which HS only drops from 94.7% to 82.3%. Ablations show hybrid generation is necessary: LLM-only adulterants are 79.3% undetected against structural detection but weaker overall; link-prediction-only is 71.3% undetected against semantic detection but weaker against structural; both baseline RR values drop as low as 60.2% under advanced attacks versus AURA's combined resilience. Impact-driven (SDS) selection outperforms random selection by 10-15 percentage points of HS at matched 100% RR. The Malatya-centrality heuristic finds a smaller vertex cover (19,924 nodes) than five competing heuristics (20,143-20,168 nodes) on the 1.76M-node WebQSP graph. On advanced GraphRAG frameworks, ARR remains 100% and HS exceeds 91% for both GraphRAG-M and LlamaIndex across datasets (Table VIII).
core_contribution: The paper proposes AURA (Active Utility Reduction via Adulteration), a defense that protects proprietary knowledge graphs (KGs) powering GraphRAG systems against IP theft by an attacker who deploys a stolen copy in a private, offline GraphRAG system. Rather than trying to prevent theft (encryption) or detect misuse after the fact (watermarking), AURA pre-emptively injects plausible-but-false 'adulterant' nodes and edges into the KG so that any unauthorized copy retrieves corrupted context and produces factually wrong LLM answers, while an authorized user holding a secret key can deterministically filter the adulterants via encrypted per-node/per-edge metadata tags and recover the original, unaltered answers. The pipeline has four stages: (1) MVC-based key-node selection -- formalizing selection of the minimal node set needed to influence every edge as a Minimum Vertex Cover problem, solved exactly via ILP for graphs up to ~150,000 nodes and via a Malatya-centrality greedy heuristic for larger graphs; (2) hybrid adulterant generation -- link-prediction models (e.g., TransE) generate structurally plausible false edges, and an LLM generates semantically coherent false nodes that replicate a key node's local neighborhood; (3) impact-driven selection -- a Semantic Deviation Score (SDS), the embedding-space distance between LLM answers generated with vs. without a candidate adulterant, is used to keep only the single most disruptive adulterant per key node; (4) a cryptographic fidelity mechanism -- AES-encrypted 'remark'/'annotation' metadata flags on every node/edge, decryptable only with the owner's secret key, that let authorized systems hierarchically filter out adulterants post-retrieval, with a stated IND-CPA-based reduction of the scheme's indistinguishability to the security of AES.
threat_model: Post-theft, private-use scenario: an attacker (external intruder or malicious insider) has already exfiltrated a copy of the proprietary KG and deploys it in their own offline/private GraphRAG system using retriever and LLM components of their choosing. The attacker cannot access the owner's secret key and is assumed not to invest in expert-driven manual KG sanitization (the paper argues this would be self-defeating, since a team capable of expert validation could just build a competing KG from scratch), but may apply standard automated sanitization: structural anomaly detection (e.g., ODDBALL), semantic-consistency detection (e.g., Node2Vec-based checks), a hybrid structural+semantic attack (SEKA), or a KGE-based (TransE) plausibility-scoring sanitization attack. Watermarking is explicitly noted as ineffective in this threat model because the owner never observes the attacker's outputs, and heavy encryption is rejected as impractical due to GraphRAG's low-latency requirements. The paper separately discusses (in the Discussion section, not as part of the core evaluated threat model) an authorized-insider knowledge-graph distillation attack that queries a live, legitimate API to reconstruct the clean KG, arguing it is mitigated operationally (internal-only APIs, anomalous query-volume detectability) rather than defended against by AURA itself.
limitations: The authors explicitly state the method adulterates only structural KG components (nodes and edges), not natural-language entity descriptions, leaving that as future work. HS is not a perfect 100% because an LLM can sometimes resolve a fact-vs-adulterant conflict using its own internal/parametric knowledge (illustrated with a movie-release-year example); the authors argue this actually understates real-world effectiveness since proprietary domain KGs are unlikely to overlap with an LLM's pretraining knowledge. The KGE-based sanitization evaluation trains the attacker's TransE model directly on the untainted original KG, which the authors themselves flag as 'impossible in practice' and note is a best-case, worst-condition test for the defense (a real attacker would only have their own incomplete collected data). The paper discusses but does not empirically evaluate a knowledge-graph distillation attack by an authorized insider with legitimate API access, relying instead on an operational (rate-limiting/anomaly-monitoring) argument rather than a technical defense within AURA itself. Latency overhead, while generally low, reaches 45.9% for retrieve-time alone on WebQSP, the largest tested graph, indicating overhead may scale with graph size. All datasets are academic KGQA benchmarks (MetaQA, WebQSP, FB15k-237, HotpotQA-derived), not an actual deployed proprietary industrial KG.

datasets_used: HotpotQA (KG extracted via LLM triple extraction)
quantitative_results: Harmfulness Score (HS) 94.3%-95.6% across 4 datasets x 4 LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-flash, Qwen-2.5-7B, Llama2-7B), Table I
Adulterant Retrieval Rate (ARR) 100% in every tested configuration, Table I
Multi-hop HS on MetaQA: 94.7% (1-hop), 95.1% (2-hop), 95.8% (3-hop), Table II
CDPA and CIRA both 100% for authorized users on all 4 datasets and 1/2/3-hop questions, Tables III-IV
Total query time increase for AURA vs. clean KG: 1.20% (MetaQA), 14.05% (WebQSP), 1.50% (FB15k-237), 2.98% (HotpotQA), Table V
AES decryption cost: 0.0095 ms per flag
Stealthiness on MetaQA (252,145 injected triples): 4.1% detected by structural anomaly detection (ODDBALL), 3.3% by semantic-consistency detection, 5.5% unique detection overall, Table VI
Robustness on MetaQA: SEKA hybrid attack removes 5.5% of adulterants; TransE-based KGE sanitization (trained on clean original KG) removes 19.8% (RR = 80.2%), after which HS drops from 94.7% to 82.3%

Ablation: LLM-only adulterants 79.3% undetected vs. structural detection; link-prediction-only 71.3% undetected vs. semantic detection; both baselines' RR drops as low as 60.2% under advanced attacks vs. AURA's ~94.5% RR under the hybrid attack and 80.2% under the KGE attack
Impact-driven (SDS) selection yields 10-15 percentage-point higher HS than random selection at matched 100% RR

MVC heuristic comparison on WebQSP (1.76M nodes): Malatya-based greedy finds vertex cover of 19,924 nodes vs. 20,143-20,168 for five competing heuristics (Edge Cover Greedy, Degree Centrality Greedy, Beam Search, Local Search, Simulated Annealing), Table VII
Link-prediction backbone choice (TransE/RotatE/ConvE) causes HS fluctuations under 0.3%
Single adulterant per key node already yields HS above 94%; additional adulterants per node give only marginal gains
Advanced GraphRAG frameworks (GraphRAG-M, LlamaIndex): ARR 100%, HS exceeds 91% across MetaQA/WebQSP/HotpotQA, Table VIII
baselines_compared: fLLM-only adulterant generation (ablation, no link-prediction component)
fLP-only adulterant generation (ablation, no LLM component)
Random Selection (ablation, vs. SDS-based impact-driven selection)
Edge Cover Greedy, Degree Centrality Greedy, Beam Search, Local Search, Simulated Annealing (MVC heuristic comparison)
GraphRAG-M (Microsoft GraphRAG) and LlamaIndex (advanced GraphRAG system baselines)
